title: White House Gates GPT-5.6, Trump Threatens 100% Tariffs on Digital Tax Countries, and G7 Scrambles on Minerals
subhead: The US government is now directly controlling which organizations can access frontier AI models, a 100% tariff threat reshapes digital trade, and the G7 tries to counter China's mineral grip as Beijing tightens state control over resources.

sections.heading: White House Gates GPT-5.6 Release, Following Anthropic Precedent
sections.body:
OpenAI released GPT-5.6 in limited preview this week — but the Trump administration dictated who gets access. The Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy coordinated with OpenAI on a per-customer vetting process, meaning individual organizations need government approval before they can use the model. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told staff the arrangement is temporary and not the company's preferred approach, according to The Information.

The arrangement mirrors, but differs from, the Anthropic precedent. Anthropic voluntarily restricted Claude Mythos 5 in April, then the Commerce Department forced Claude Fable 5 offline entirely on June 12. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick partially lifted the Mythos 5 restriction on June 26, allowing deployment for critical infrastructure defense organizations only. Fable 5 remains suspended worldwide. Both triggers involved cybersecurity concerns: Amazon researchers demonstrated a classifier bypass on Fable 5, and separately, NSA Director Gen. Joshua Rudd briefed that Mythos 5 penetrated classified NSA systems in a red-team exercise 'not in weeks, but in hours,' per The Economist.

The legal basis is shaky. Trump's June 2 executive order describes a voluntary 30-day pre-release review window and explicitly states it 'should not be interpreted as authorizing any form of mandatory licensing.' Yet three weeks later, the government is directly controlling model access, and companies are complying. Brad Carson of Public First described the approach to CNN as 'ad hoc and potentially lawless.' No formal benchmarks or submission process exists yet for determining which models qualify as covered frontier models.
sections.whyItMatters: The US government is de facto controlling frontier AI model access without clear legal authority, setting a pattern that could become permanent. Two of the three leading AI companies are now operating under government-imposed release restrictions within the same month.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens 100% Tariff on Digital Services Tax Countries
sections.body:
President Trump threatened on June 27 a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on American technology companies, Reuters reported. The threat came one day after EU countries met Trump's July 4 deadline to cut tariffs on US goods.

The move targets a longstanding frustration for US tech companies. Multiple European countries — including France, Italy, Spain, and the UK — already operate digital services taxes that disproportionately affect American firms. The threat effectively forces allies to choose between their digital tax revenue and access to the US market for all their exports.

Reuters described the threat as inflaming transatlantic trade tensions. The scope is broad: a 100% tariff on all goods from any imposing country would dwarf existing tariff levels and likely violate WTO rules, though the administration has shown little concern for WTO constraints.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most aggressive US trade action specifically targeting digital tax policy to date. It forces US allies into an impossible choice between sovereign tax policy and market access, and could reshape how governments worldwide tax tech platforms.

sections.heading: G7 Moves on Critical Minerals as China Tightens State Control
sections.body:
G7 leaders meeting in France launched an action plan to secure critical mineral supply chains, pledging to develop a road map by end of 2026 for 'standards-based markets' for rare earth elements and other critical minerals. China controls roughly 70% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing capacity, according to Newsweek.

Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi proposed a G7 joint stockpiling framework for critical minerals, the Japan Times reported. The proposal came as China tightened export restrictions on rare earths — first against the US during last year's trade dispute, and more recently against Japan following Takaichi's comments on Taiwan, per Newsweek. New Chinese regulations that took effect June 23 establish a comprehensive state reserve system and grant the State Council greater authority over exploration, mining, processing, storage, and trade of mineral resources.

Trump failed to secure European backing for a Pentagon-drafted plan to establish floor prices for critical minerals. European governments called for studies first, concerned about market distortions. The G7 statement acknowledged that 'non-market policies and practices in the critical minerals sector threaten our ability to acquire many critical minerals.' China's embassy in Washington told Newsweek its export controls are 'consistent with international norms.'
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is building institutions to reduce dependence on Chinese minerals, but without floor price commitments, the plan lacks teeth. Meanwhile China is moving its mineral economy further under direct state control, giving Beijing even more leverage as a strategic weapon.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms for Eight Months
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has delayed adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and over 100 Chinese firms to the Entity List for nearly eight months — the longest gap in updates in over a decade, CryptoBriefing reported. An interagency committee approved the designations, but the Trump administration is holding implementation to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing during trade negotiations.

At least 75 of the delayed firms are connected to advanced semiconductor manufacturing and AI technology supply chains. DeepSeek, which built competitive AI systems at a fraction of Western costs, has been accused of evading export controls via shell companies to access restricted Nvidia chips. CXMT is China's most advanced domestic DRAM manufacturer, designated by the Pentagon as a Chinese military company. Meanwhile, the Pentagon added Alibaba, Baidu, BYD, NIO, and Unitree to its list of Chinese military companies on June 8, bringing the total to 188, Reuters reported. Alibaba has since sued the Pentagon over the designation.

The result is a growing gap between what US national security reviewers have determined and what the administration is willing to enforce. National security critics argue the freeze creates a window for US technology to continue flowing to flagged firms.
sections.whyItMatters: There is now a significant gap between US threat assessments and enforcement actions. Firms the government's own review process flagged as problematic continue receiving American technology, while the administration prioritizes trade negotiations.

sections.heading: Chip Security Act Gains Industry Support for GPS-Tracking AI Exports
sections.body:
Six companies that track international technology shipments sent a letter to congressional leaders supporting the Chip Security Act, which would require location-tracking mechanisms for exported advanced AI chips, NBC News reported. The bill passed the House Foreign Affairs Committee 42-0.

The legislation targets a specific vulnerability in current export controls: US law already prohibits selling advanced AI chips to China, but third-country diversion remains rampant. In March, the Justice Department charged three people with conspiring to divert approximately $2.5 billion worth of AI servers to China. The tracking system could use specially designed hardware or software on existing equipment, and supporters argue it would let the US sell chips to countries like Malaysia and Indonesia with less diversion risk.

Dutch officials are simultaneously pressing US lawmakers about the bill's implications. A key Dutch minister met with US Commerce officials on June 23 regarding chip export legislation, Reuters reported — significant because the Netherlands controls ASML, the only company producing extreme ultraviolet lithography machines essential for advanced chipmaking.

The semiconductor industry's main lobbying groups oppose the requirements, arguing they could restrict the rapidly growing US chip industry. But the six tracking companies argue stronger verification would increase confidence, leading to faster approvals and more sales.
sections.whyItMatters: For the first time, there is serious bipartisan momentum for physically tracking AI chip exports. If passed, this would close the biggest loophole in chip export controls — third-country diversion — and reshape how the global semiconductor trade operates.

sections.heading: Singapore Electronics Exports Surge 94.8% as AI Demand Reshapes Trade Flows
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 38.4% year-on-year in May, the sharpest growth in over two decades, Enterprise Singapore reported. Electronics exports led, surging 94.8%, with integrated circuits up 80.9%, disk media products up 227.8%, and PCs up 140.9%.

EToro analyst Zavier Wong traced the surge to a 'single concentrated force': Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively raising their 2026 capital expenditure plans above $700 billion. Singapore's exports to Taiwan grew 135.2%, to the US 80.9%, and to China 31%. Singapore Semiconductor Industry Association executive director Ang Wee Seng noted that while not every dollar is AI-related, 'a significant portion of the electronics uplift is either directly or indirectly connected to AI demand.'

The concentration risk is clear. Singapore's export growth is now heavily dependent on the capex decisions of four American companies. If hyperscaler spending slows, the effect on Singapore's economy would be immediate and severe.
sections.whyItMatters: AI infrastructure spending is creating dramatic trade distortions. Singapore's economy is now acutely leveraged to the capex plans of four US companies, and any pullback in AI investment would hit its export sector hard.

sections.heading: China Mass-Produces Cheap Stealth Coating, Pentagon Adds Unitree to Military List
sections.body:
A team from Foshan University reported a low-cost microwave-absorbing composite made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide that could make stealth coatings cheap enough for mass production, SCMP reported. The material, published in the Journal of Ceramics in April, replaces expensive graphene and carbon nanotubes with far cheaper expanded graphite — potentially broadening stealth technology beyond niche military applications. Separately, China is marketing its J-35 stealth fighter to Middle Eastern buyers, according to MSN.

On the US side, the Pentagon expanded its Chinese military companies list to 188 firms on June 8, adding Alibaba, Baidu, BYD, NIO, and robotics company Unitree, Reuters reported. The list bars these firms from US defense contracts. The Pentagon had released and then quickly pulled an earlier version without explanation. Alibaba has sued to be removed.
sections.whyItMatters: China is pushing military technology toward mass production economics that the US cannot match, while the US continues expanding its list of designated military-linked firms. The Foshan University research, if scaled, could make radar-absorbing materials a commodity rather than a premium military technology.

bottomLine: The US government is now directly controlling frontier AI model access without clear legal authority — both Anthropic and OpenAI are operating under government-imposed release restrictions this month. Trump's 100% tariff threat on digital services tax countries escalates a new front in trade warfare, targeting sovereign tax policy rather than just goods. The G7 committed to a critical minerals road map but failed to agree on floor prices, leaving the plan without its most powerful tool even as China consolidates state control over mineral resources. Eight months of Entity List delays mean over 100 flagged Chinese firms continue receiving American technology. The Chip Security Act, if passed, would fundamentally change how the US enforces semiconductor export controls. Across all these developments, the pattern is the same: the institutions designed to manage tech-power competition are struggling to keep pace with how fast the technology and the politics are moving.
sources: Reuters, MSN, CryptoBriefing, MemeBurn, TechTimes, CNBC, NBC News, ProPakistani, Newsweek, Japan Times, SCMP, Channel News Asia, The Information, Axios, CNN, The Economist, Fortune, Wall Street Journal, Simple Flying, Startup Fortune, NDTV, The Australian, Yahoo News, Digitimes
